title: Opioid Oversedation & Naloxone
subtitle: 54 y/o female post-op on a morphine PCA, now difficult to arouse
nursesNote: 0300 - 54 y/o female, post-op day 0 after abdominal surgery, using a morphine patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) pump for pain. On rounds the nurse finds her difficult to arouse, responding only to vigorous stimulation. Respirations are slow and shallow at 7/min, SpO2 has fallen to 88% on room air, and her pupils are pinpoint. Her adult daughter at the bedside states, 'She was hurting, so I kept pressing the button for her while she slept.'

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) findings that BEST indicate opioid-induced respiratory depression.

opts.text: Respiratory rate of 7/min, slow and shallow
opts.rat: A low respiratory rate with shallow breathing is the hallmark of opioid-induced respiratory depression.

opts.text: Difficult to arouse, responding only to vigorous stimulation
opts.rat: Excessive sedation-a decreased level of consciousness-precedes and accompanies opioid respiratory depression.

opts.text: Pinpoint (constricted) pupils
opts.rat: Miosis (pinpoint pupils) is a classic sign of opioid effect.

opts.text: SpO2 fallen to 88%
opts.rat: A falling oxygen saturation reflects hypoventilation from the opioid effect.

opts.text: Blood pressure 112/68
opts.rat: This blood pressure is near baseline and not the key sign of opioid oversedation.

opts.text: Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
opts.rat: A mildly low hemoglobin from surgery is not a sign of opioid respiratory depression.

opts.text: Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
opts.rat: Normal renal function is not a sign of oversedation.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly interpret the situation.

opts.text: The daughter pressing the PCA button ('PCA by proxy') has delivered extra opioid the client did not self-administer
opts.rat: Only the client should press the PCA button; dosing by family or staff bypasses the built-in safety of self-administration and can cause overdose.

opts.text: The ABG (low pH, high PaCO2) shows respiratory acidosis from hypoventilation
opts.rat: Opioid-induced hypoventilation retains CO2, producing a respiratory acidosis (low pH, high PaCO2).

opts.text: Naloxone is the opioid antagonist that can reverse the respiratory depression
opts.rat: Naloxone reverses opioid effects and is used for significant opioid-induced respiratory depression.

opts.text: The safest response is to press the PCA button more to keep her comfortable
opts.rat: Giving more opioid would deepen the respiratory depression; the opioid must be stopped, not increased.

opts.text: Pinpoint pupils suggest a stimulant overdose
opts.rat: Stimulants dilate pupils; pinpoint pupils point to opioids.

opts.text: These findings are expected and need no intervention
opts.rat: A respiratory rate of 7 with an SpO2 of 88% is dangerous and requires immediate action.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the explanations from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Opioid-induced respiratory depression from excess PCA dosing (by proxy)
opts.rat: Slow breathing, sedation, pinpoint pupils, and the reported proxy dosing make opioid oversedation the clear cause.

opts.text: Postoperative hypoventilation worsened by the opioid
opts.rat: Post-op splinting/atelectasis can contribute, but the opioid effect is the dominant, reversible cause here.

opts.text: Oversedation compounded by the client's own drowsiness during sleep
opts.rat: Sleep lowers respiratory drive somewhat, but the opioid excess is the key issue.

opts.text: Acute stroke as the primary cause
opts.rat: Pinpoint pupils with reversible opioid signs make stroke much less likely; treat the opioid effect first.

opts.text: Simple normal post-op sleep
opts.rat: Least likely: an RR of 7 and SpO2 of 88% are not normal sleep and require intervention.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) MOST appropriate immediate interventions.

opts.text: Stop the PCA opioid and stimulate the client while assessing airway and breathing
opts.rat: Stopping the opioid and stimulating the client, with airway/breathing support, are the immediate priorities.

opts.text: Apply oxygen and support ventilation as needed
opts.rat: Oxygen and ventilatory support correct the hypoxemia while the opioid effect is addressed.

opts.text: Call for help and administer naloxone per order/protocol
opts.rat: Naloxone reverses the opioid-induced respiratory depression and is given per order for significant depression.

opts.text: Teach the family that only the client may press the PCA button, and monitor for re-sedation
opts.rat: Educating the family prevents recurrence, and monitoring is needed because naloxone is short-acting and re-sedation can occur.

opts.text: Press the PCA button to give another dose for pain
opts.rat: Additional opioid would worsen the respiratory depression.

opts.text: Place the client flat and leave the room to get supplies
opts.rat: Leaving a client with RR 7 and SpO2 88% is unsafe; stay and call for help.

opts.text: Administer a large single dose of naloxone to fully reverse all opioid effect at once
opts.rat: Naloxone is titrated to restore adequate breathing; a large abrupt dose can precipitate severe pain and acute withdrawal.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Stop the PCA and stimulate the client while checking airway/breathing
opts.rat: FIRST-stopping the opioid and assessing/stimulating addresses the immediate threat.

opts.text: Apply oxygen and support ventilation
opts.rat: SECOND-oxygenation is restored while further help is summoned.

opts.text: Call for help and notify the provider
opts.rat: THIRD-additional responders and orders (including naloxone) are mobilized.

opts.text: Administer naloxone titrated to restore adequate respirations
opts.rat: FOURTH-the antagonist reverses the respiratory depression, titrated to effect.

opts.text: Continue close monitoring for re-sedation and educate the family about PCA use
opts.rat: LAST-ongoing monitoring catches re-sedation, and teaching prevents recurrence.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After naloxone and supportive care, classify each finding as Improving, Worsening, or Unchanged.

opts.text: Respiratory rate rises to 16 and SpO2 improves to 96%
opts.rat: A normal respiratory rate and oxygen saturation show the opioid effect has been reversed.

opts.text: The client becomes easily arousable and oriented
opts.rat: Returning alertness indicates the respiratory depression and oversedation have resolved.

opts.text: The family verbalizes that only the client may press the PCA button
opts.rat: Correct understanding reduces the risk of another proxy-dosing event.

opts.text: Thirty minutes later the client becomes drowsy again with an RR of 8
opts.rat: Re-sedation is expected because naloxone is shorter-acting than the opioid; repeat naloxone/monitoring is needed.

opts.text: SpO2 falls again to 85% with shallow breathing
opts.rat: A falling oxygen saturation with shallow breathing signals recurring respiratory depression requiring re-intervention.

opts.text: The client reports pain of 7/10 but is breathing 16/min with SpO2 97%
opts.rat: Adequate breathing with pain reflects the expected trade-off after reversal; pain is managed with safer, monitored analgesia-neither a decline nor full resolution of comfort.

opts.text: Blood pressure remains 114/70 with no change
opts.rat: A stable blood pressure is neither an improvement nor a worsening in this situation.

opts.text: Pupils remain pinpoint with no return of alertness
opts.rat: Persistent pinpoint pupils and unchanged sedation indicate the opioid effect has not yet been reversed and further naloxone is needed.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
